skill.name: prpm-json-best-practices
skill.description: Guides authors through creating valid prpm.json manifests for PRPM packages, collections, multi-package repositories, and publishing workflows.
skill.summary: Create complete PRPM package manifests with schema-aware guidance for packages, collections, and publishing workflows.
skill.author: AgentWorkforce

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, and credential patterns, but review shows most are documentation examples, schema examples, or literal field names. The confirmed risk is moderate because the skill documents PRPM lifecycle scripts that can execute shell commands when copied into a manifest and run by publishing tools. No prompt injection attempt, data exfiltration intent, or malicious code was found.

medium_findings.title: Lifecycle Script Guidance Can Lead to Shell Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill documents PRPM lifecycle scripts and shows shell command strings for build and publish workflows. This is a legitimate package manifest feature, but copied commands execute in the user environment during publishing and require review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script examples are explicit command strings in manifest documentation. The use is legitimate, but the execution behavior is real if users adopt the examples.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: The reported URLs are schema identifiers, repository examples, or documentation links. No code performs outbound network requests or sends local data to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are static JSON strings or prose references. There is no executable network client or exfiltration path in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged many ordinary schema fields, Markdown terms, and example manifest values as weak cryptography. No hashing, encryption, signing, or password storage implementation was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged lines contain descriptions, manifest metadata, or validation patterns. They do not implement or recommend cryptographic algorithms.

low_findings.title: Credential Placeholder Is a Configuration Example
low_findings.description: The DATABASE_URL references are placeholders inside conversion hint examples. They do not read environment variables or transmit secrets, but users should avoid committing real connection strings.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are literal placeholders in Markdown JSON examples. The residual risk is user misuse, not malicious behavior in the skill.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Findings Are Path Examples
low_findings.description: The filesystem alerts point to validation examples, relative path examples, and an explicit warning against absolute paths. The skill does not access files itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is documentation or schema data, not file I/O code. One relative traversal appears in a shell example and is covered by the medium script finding.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Is Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The combined network, command, filesystem, and credential heuristic is caused by examples coexisting in one documentation skill. No code combines these capabilities to collect credentials, access files, or exfiltrate data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review confirmed the suspicious patterns are separated documentation examples. There is no executable implementation that joins them into a malicious workflow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Manifest Scripts With Shell Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill explains script fields that run commands during package operations. Users should inspect generated scripts before publishing or running PRPM workflows.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command strings are present and executable if copied into manifests. The pattern is documented as normal publishing behavior, not hidden execution.

content.value_statement: PRPM package authors need valid manifests for packages, collections, and multi-package repositories. This skill provides structured guidance for required fields, file lists, tags, conversion hints, and publishing checks.
actual_capabilities: Explains required prpm.json fields for single-package manifests.
Shows how to structure multi-package repositories with package arrays.
Describes collection manifests that reference local and external packages.
Documents tags, categories, organization fields, and package metadata.
Covers conversion hints for Claude, Cursor, Kiro, Copilot, and related formats.
Provides publishing workflow checks for validation, duplicates, and file existence.
limitations: Does not validate manifests automatically unless the user runs separate tools.
Does not publish packages or install dependencies by itself.
Examples use placeholder names, URLs, and package identifiers.
Lifecycle script guidance requires manual command review before use.

use_cases.title: Prepare a New Skill Package
use_cases.description: Create a complete manifest with name, version, format, subtype, tags, license, and file entries.

use_cases.title: Organize a Multi-Package Repository
use_cases.description: Design a repository manifest that publishes several related skills, agents, rules, or prompts together.

use_cases.title: Build a Curated Package Collection
use_cases.description: Define collections with package IDs, version ranges, required flags, and clear inclusion reasons.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Manifest
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a prpm.json manifest for one Claude skill. Ask for any missing required fields before drafting it.

prompt_templates.title: Review Manifest Completeness
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my prpm.json for missing required fields, weak descriptions, invalid tags, duplicate names, and file path issues.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Multi-Package Manifest
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a PRPM multi-package manifest for these skills, agents, and rules. Group metadata clearly and flag publish risks.

prompt_templates.title: Add Collections and Conversion Hints
prompt_templates.prompt: Extend this PRPM manifest with collections, version ranges, conversion hints, and safe lifecycle script recommendations.

output_examples.input: A user wants to publish one Claude Code skill from a repository.
output: A checklist of required manifest fields.
Recommended package name, format, subtype, tags, and file path guidance.
Warnings about placeholder values and file verification before publishing.

output_examples.input: A maintainer has several rules and skills in one repository.
output: Naming recommendations that avoid duplicate package IDs.
Guidance for private packages, tags, and publishing order.

output_examples.input: A curator wants a collection for testing tools.
output: A collection structure with package references and version ranges.
Short reasons that explain each package inclusion.

best_practices: Use clear package names, descriptions, tags, and SPDX license identifiers.
Verify every file path before publishing a manifest.
Review lifecycle scripts manually before running publish workflows.
anti_patterns: Do not create prpm.json files for packages you only install as a user.
Do not mix simple file strings and file objects in one files array.
Do not store secrets or absolute local paths in manifests.

faq.question: What does this skill help me create?
faq.answer: It helps create and review prpm.json manifests for PRPM packages, collections, and multi-package repositories.

faq.question: Does it publish packages for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides manifest guidance. Publishing still requires the PRPM command-line tools and user approval.

faq.question: Can it help with Claude Code packages?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers Claude and Claude Code formats, including skills, agents, hooks, and related metadata.

faq.question: Can it validate my existing manifest?
faq.answer: It can review structure and likely issues, but formal validation requires a JSON schema validator or PRPM tooling.

faq.question: Why are lifecycle scripts a risk?
faq.answer: Lifecycle scripts can run shell commands during package operations. Review every generated command before using it.

faq.question: Should secrets go in prpm.json?
faq.answer: No. Use environment placeholders only when needed, and never commit real tokens or database connection strings.
